content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814
June 1, 1977

James R. Christiansen
City of Carpinteria
1216 State St., Suite 802
Santa Barbara, CA 93101

Dear Mr. Christiansen:

Thank you for your letter of January 7, 1977, requesting informal advice concerning whether Charles Dibble, a member of the city council for the City of Carpinteria, can participate in a council decision under the following circumstances. Mr. Dibble is a practicing attorney and is involved in a partnership with a younger attorney, who is his junior partner. A corporate owner of an apartment house in Carpinteria recently settled certain litigation with the city, subject to the condition that a proposed beachfront development be approved by the State Lands Commission, the California Coastal Zone Commission and the city council of Carpinteria. Mr. Dibble would like to participate in the city council's decision. He is concerned, however, about his ability to do so if his junior partner agrees to represent the corporate owner of the apartment house on another legal matter unrelated to the aforementioned litigation which would generate legal fees in excess of $250.

In order to avoid the potential conflict of interest confronting Mr. Dibble, the junior partner would like to represent the corporate client on an independent contractor basis and not involve the partnership. Moreover, to insure the independent nature of the arrangement, the junior partner and Mr. Dibble would agree that no portion of the fee paid by the corporate client to the junior partner would be paid or allocated to Mr. Dibble, nor would any portion of the fee be applied toward the partnership's overhead or expenses.

Finally, you have informed me that there is a written partnership agreement which existed prior to the time the corporate client sought representation by the junior partner. This agreement specifies the percentage of the partnership's profits that will be allocated to Mr. Dibble and to the junior partner.

James R. Christiansen
June 1, 1977
Page Two


     The basic conflict of interest prohibition in the Political Reform Act provides that a public official may not make, participate in making or in any way use his official position to influence a governmental decision in which he has a financial interest. Gov. Code Section 87100.1/ An official has a financial interest in a governmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on a source of income the official receives of $250 or more. Section 87103.2/ Accordingly, the initial issue posed by your inquiry on behalf of Mr. Dibble is whether the corporate owner would become a source of income to Mr. Dibble if the junior partner represented the client under the circumstances outlined above.

     The definition of the term income provides, in pertinent part, that income of an individual "includes a pro rata share of any income of any business entity or trust in which an individual ... owns, directly, indirectly or beneficially, a 10 percent interest or greater." Section 82030(a). Mr. Dibble's law partnership is a business entity (Section 82005) and we assume, for purposes of rendering this informal advice, that he has a 10 percent or greater interest in the partnership and that his pro rata share of the fees paid by the corporate client, if the partnership were to represent the corporation, would exceed $250.

     Pursuant to the arrangement proposed by the junior partner, the partnership will not represent the corporate client and will not derive any income or benefits by reason of the junior partner's representation of the corporate client. We conclude that under these circumstances Mr. Dibble will not derive a pro rata share of the corporate client's fee and, hence, the corporate client will not become a source of income to him.

     This conclusion is based on the following factors. Under the arrangement proposed by Mr. Dibble and his junior partner, the junior partner will represent the corporation as an independent contractor and will retain the fee he earns. The partnership will not receive any of the fee and

___________________________

1/ All references are to the Government Code unless otherwise noted.

2/ The Act specifies other types of financial interests in addition to sources of income (such as investments and interests in real property) but they are not relevant to your inquiry. See Section 87103.

.
James R. Christiansen
June 1, 1977
Page Three


no part of the fee will be applied to the partnership's
overhead or expenses. Moreover, there is an existing written
agreement which allocates the profits of the partnership and
we assume that this agreement cannot be manipulated and will
not be altered to compensate Mr. Dibble for the lost income
resulting from the junior partner's separate representation
of the corporate client. We further assume that no other
means of compensating Mr. Dibble for the lost income outside
of the context of the partnership agreement will be employed,
such as increasing his annual bonus.

Under these circumstances, Mr. Dibble will not derive
any financial benefit from the corporate client's fee, either
by reason of his partnership status or individually, and
hence the corporate client will not be a source of income to
him. Mr. Dibble, therefore, will not have the requisite
financial interest in the corporate client and, consequently,
will not be precluded by the Political Reform Act from par­
ticipating in decisions which have a financial effect on the
corporation.

We add to this conclusion, however, two caveats. First,
our conclusion herein is limited to the unique facts presented
and does not represent a general endorsement of the idea
that potential conflicts of interest confronting attorney-
public officials always can be negated by arrangements to
sever clients from the attorney's law firm. Each such ar­
rangement will have to be carefully scrutinized to determine
whether the attorney, in fact, will derive no financial
benefit from the client. Second, we intimate no opinion
concerning whether participation by Mr. Dibble in a decision
involving a client represented by his junior partner, albeit
as an independent contractor, is ethical. We suggest, there­
fore, that before advising Mr. Dibble you consult the applicable
rules of the State Bar of California and the American Bar
Association's Code of Professional Responsibility.

3/ It should be noted that although the corporation
will not be a source of income to Mr. Dibble, Mr. Dibble's
law partnership is a source of income. Accordingly, if it
were reasonably foreseeable that a decision involving the
corporation would have a material financial effect on Mr. Dibble's
law partnership, he would have to disqualify himself from
participating pursuant to Section 87100. One way in which
this could occur, for example, is if existing clients of the
partnership threatened to remove their business if Mr. Dibble
made a certain decision with respect to the corporate client
represented by his junior partner.

James R. Christiansen
June 1, 1977
Page Four


As a second question, you asked how a partner in a law
firm determines when the $250 threshold specified in Section
87103(c) is satisfied. A client of a law firm becomes a
source of income to a partner in the firm if the partner has
a 10 percent or greater interest in the firm and his pro
rata share of the client's gross fee is $250 or more. Thus,
if a partner has a 25 percent interest in a law firm, a
client must pay the firm a $1,000 fee or greater to become a
source of income to the partner.

Thank you for your interest in the Political Reform
Act. If you have any further questions, please do not hesi-
tate to contact me.

Sincerely,

Ted Prim
Ted Prim
Staff Counsel
Legal Division

TP:KHF:plh

CITY of CARPINTERIA

1216 State St., Suite 802, Santa Barbara, CA 93101
(805) 962-8141

January 7, 1977

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

Gentlemen:

I am writing to you to request an informal written
opinion regarding a potential conflict of interest
problem. It arises out and concerns a city councilman of the city who is also a practicing attorney-at-
law with offices in the city and in partnership with
a younger attorney, who is the councilman's junior
partner.

In the past, the city has had extensive litigation
with the corporate-owner of an apartment house involving ownership of certain beach-front property.
The city recently conditionally settled that litigation based upon the approval of certain beach front
development. The city council feels the project will
up-grade the existing development. Final settlement
has not yet been made due to the condition, although
the State Lands Commission and the city council
have approved the settlement. The matter must now go
before the California Coastal Zone Conservation Commission, where it is anticipated that certain changes
will be made in the proposed project. Such an action
will require a vote of concurrence by the city council.
The city councilman-lawyer desires not to be disqualified on his future vote on this matter. However, the
corporate-owner of this apartment house has requested
the city councilman's junior partner represent it in
legal matters having nothing to do with the litigation
with the city nor the city's future approval of any
amendment to the developemnt plan. The city councilman's junior partner would like to take this case, as
an independent contractor on his own, without involving
the legal partnership in any way.

Accordingly, the question is as follows: "Would a city
councilman-attorney be disqualified in voting on an
application when he is a legal partnership with another
attorney, and that other attorney in the last few
months has taken a case as an independent contractor
(and not as a part of the partnership) involving a fee

Fair Political Practices Commission
January 7, 1977
Page Two.

in excess of $250.00 on a matter involving the applicant?"
In asking this question, it is assumed that none of the
share of said fee to the other attorney will be paid to
nor allocated to the councilman-attorney nor any of the
partnership's office overhead or expenses.

As a further question, I would appreciate it very much
if you could advise me as to whether a lawyer is in a
partnership with another attorney, the "$250.00 fee"
disqualification amount is met when $250.00 is paid to
the partnership within the last 12 months, or when the
city councilmen's share of the partnership gross profits
is more than $250.00.

Your informal written advise to this matter would be
very much appreciated.

Very sincerely yours,

James R. Christiansen

JRC/dp

sections.question: Would a city councilman-attorney be disqualified in voting on an application when he is a legal partnership with another attorney, and that other attorney in the last few months has taken a case as an independent contractor (and not as a part of the partnership) involving a fee in excess of $250.00 on a matter involving the applicant? As a further question, when a lawyer is in a partnership with another attorney, is the "$250.00 fee" disqualification amount met when $250.00 is paid to the partnership within the last 12 months, or when the city councilmen's share of the partnership gross profits is more than $250.00?
sections.question_synthetic: The inquiry seeks to determine whether a city councilman who is an attorney in a law partnership would be disqualified from voting on an application involving a party that has recently retained the councilman's junior partner as an independent contractor for unrelated legal work exceeding $250 in fees. The question also asks how the $250 disqualification threshold applies in partnership situations—whether it is measured by total partnership fees received or the individual councilman's share of partnership profits.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter, not an FPPC response. No CONCLUSION or SHORT ANSWER section is present. Document contains two related questions about disqualification thresholds in partnership scenarios. Some OCR artifacts present but core content is legible.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: Would a city councilman-attorney be disqualified in voting on an application when he is a legal partnership with another attorney, and that other attorney in the last few months has taken a case as an independent contractor (and not as a part of the partnership) involving a fee in excess of $250.00 on a matter involving the applicant? As a further question, when a lawyer is in a partnership with another attorney, is the "$250.00 fee" disqualification amount met when $250.00 is paid to the partnership within the last 12 months, or when the city councilmen's share of the partnership gross profits is more than $250.00?

embedding.summary: This is an incoming request letter from a City of Carpinteria representative dated January 7, 1977, asking the FPPC for an informal written opinion on a potential conflict of interest involving a city councilman-attorney whose junior law partner has taken a case from a party with whom the city has recently settled litigation.
